speaker: interviewer
text: It is November 20, 1996. The survivor is Linda Breder, born with the last name Reich. My name is Yvonne Walter. We're in San Francisco, California, United States of America. And the language is English. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My name is Yvonne Walter. Today is November 20, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Linda Breder in San Francisco, California, United States of America. The language is English. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Good morning, Mrs. Breder.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please state your name-- your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Linda. At birth, Linda Libusha Breder.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Linda Breder.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your birth place and your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stropkov. February 24, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you spell Stropkov for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-T-R-O-P-K-O-V.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what country was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now, Mrs. Breder?

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back and talk about your childhood and your family. Tell me about your father and mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, I would like to say I was born in Slovakia, which is now a part from Czechoslovakia. And my father and my all ancestry were born in the Austro-Hungarian empire. My father got married in 1914. And right after, he was drafted to the Austria-Hungarian army. He spent in Siberia four years. After four years, when he came back, he had the little boy named Herman Hershl. And when he came back, he was sick. His toes were frozen. They had to amputate his toes on his feet. Naturally, it was not in my time, because my brother was older 10 years from me. As I know, my parents had a relatively good life. They were a middle-class family. We lived in the city, where Jews and Christians were living side by side. And we had all our families, my uncles and aunts, around in the city, in the neighboring cities, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- later on, in 1922, my other brother was born. His name was Izkach Meyer. And two years later, my mother had twins, my brother and me. And again, two years later, my sister was born. But my mother died at childbirth in 1926. So I suppose-- since I can't remember-- it was a big tragedy in our family.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather-- my oldest brother went to my grandfather. They raised him. He was this time, 10 years old. My middle brother, to the other grandparents. And my sister, my oldest and my mother's sister raised her, but not in the same city. With our father, only we twins were staying. My father re-married. I don't know exactly when, because I was a little child. And we had a grandmother, I mean, who was very, very close to us. So most of the time, we spent with our grandmother, with our grandparents. But my father, at this time, he was a leather merchant, had a store in the city. And I never knew that I-- my stepmother was not my mother. But then again-- it must be in the '30s-- my stepmother died. I don't know what was the cause. They never talked about it. And when I was about 10-- I think it was in 1934, 1933-- my father re-married again. And then he stayed with my other stepmother until the war came. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your other brothers and sisters who lived with relatives? Did you ever see them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We see each other each vacation time. The whole year, I was jumping and being happy that I'm going to visit them-- going to see my-- my little sister and my brothers. And I went from one city to another, always staying a week or two, so we could visit. But they did seldom come back home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What cities were those, Mrs. Breder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those were Presov, Kosice, Bardejov, around. Not far, in a vicinity about 100 miles each. But on the holidays, many times, the oldest brother came. Usually, we spent the holidays together with our grandparents, and, you know, other aunts and uncles. But the life was pretty good. The city was nice. We didn't hear of any pogroms or so. Never. And I hardly could feel before that anti-Semitism was in the place where we were living.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about Jewish life in your city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we belonged to an orthodox community. My father attended the shiva for 40 years, and so did all my brothers. And I, when I was three years old-- naturally, my brother had to go to a cheder-- to a Jewish school, I mean. And I was so jealous. I always wanted to be there, too. So all the time, I went in. And the rabbi-- rabbi-- not the teacher, I cannot recall-- the melamed threw me out. Not for girls. But I still was going in. And finally heard, OK, you can stay. So actually, I learned Hebrew reading and writing since I was three years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: The synagogue, you know, since we belonged to an orthodox community, only men went to the synagogue. Saturdays, although, I went, too, with my mother. And we were-- but on the balcony, so we could see the men only from up there. But we mostly played outside in the courtyard of the synagogue. It was a joyous day, Saturdays, always. I remember my stepmother always gave us a little package, because they were there about three or four hours. So we had what to eat. I can say my stepmother was so good to us. I never knew she is not our stepmother. And we went to public schools. Because in the city where we were didn't have, you know, religious schools, only Sundays and Saturdays. Or we had, twice a week, Hebrew classes. And I thought all the Jews lived this way. I had no idea that it existed, you know, Reform Jewry, because the other temple what it was in our city was conservative. So my-- I think so, three uncles went to the conservative. But my father was like my grandfather-- he stuck to the orthodoxy. And life went on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about other children with whom you played?

speaker: interviewee
text: I played with our neighbors and with our schoolchildren. We were like children in the whole world, you know-- communicated, fight we had, and then everything was OK. But, like I say, you know, our childhood, mine and my brothers, who were at home, was very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe a typical day in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a typical day in my life, you know what, like all the time in the morning, I didn't want to get up. I wanted to stay. At 8 o'clock, I had to be in school. Although the school was only across our house. And then I was rushing-- I didn't eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at break, when we had, my father brought us a hot lunch. And I was so embarrassed. Why? No one else in the school did that? But my father was a very, you know, lovable person. I think so, he brought that because we were orphans. Did you have kosher?

speaker: interviewee
text: Orthodox and not kosher-- did you hear of something like that? Sure, we were. We were kosher, strictly kosher. Not only kosher. I couldn't wear short sleeves, even. I had to wear long-- not socks, but long hosiery, I would say. And the colors of the socks and all over were also not light colors. And I wanted so badly to get rid of it, but I respected my father's wishes. Later on, I did it. I rebelled when I was already a teenager, and I threw all the dark things away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe some of the Jewish community in your village?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a very nice Jewish community. First of all, you know, we lived in this part of the city, I mean, in the city, for centuries. But I know our ancestry was already, at the 17th century, in this city. My father was born about 20 kilometers, not far away, where we used to live. My grandparents, too. And so all our lives was done, you know, in the same place. And in the house what we lived, it was built, I think so, before my father was married. And we all lived, you know, a good life, very comfortable. And I-- what I recall, it was-- my friends lived the same way. Naturally, there were people who were richer and poorer, but we played together. We didn't see any differences in the status of what, you know, other parents of my friends have. And holidays special-- was very, very, I mean, glamorous. We went to visit to special people, and we were treated well. And then, you know, on Purim, we used to go from house to house, [NON-ENGLISH]. And we always were jealous of the others. I was, at least, because they had the largest package, and I couldn't get to it, or I was not-- not enough pushy to ask for it. But what I know, it was really-- you know, looking back today, it was probably the nicest, I mean, time before the war for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your district where you lived, were there other than Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was many others. We lived side by side. Our house Jewish, then two or three Christians, then again. You know, we never were in ghettos. And I don't remember even that my parents mentioned, you know, that their grandparents also lived separated or singled out. No. In this part, I think so-- maybe it was, but I didn't notice. I can't say that even once somebody called me Jewish. I can't recall that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what can I say? It was nice, and a good life. Since, you know, I was so young before the war, I don't have so much memories like people who were already teenagers. When Hitler came, I was 14. Do you remember any of the Jewish merchants or the shochet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I remember them. Sure, I can. You know, when I came back from camp, I could point it out, the houses staring at me, who lived where, and how many children, who was my friend for this family. But since, you know, it passed already more than half a century, it's hard to recall that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you celebrate the Sabbath?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, the Sabbath. For First Friday, it must be cleaning. Everything was cleaned, though mainly the kitchen. And then we baked our challah and all of the goodies, the sweets, the breads, at home. Now, in a small town, usually, you had a special kitchen, where it was also not an oven to bake. So-- and the smell of those goodies Friday. And I always jumped around to want to help. But since, you know, the maid cleaned and everything. So I helped, and I was always wet-- even my hair were wet. Because we had wooden floors in the kitchen, and everything had to have spic-and-span. And then the baking. Then in the evening, first of all, my mother lit the candles. And I was standing on a little chair, because I was never too tall to reach the same way like she did. So I helped light the candles, and then my father went to shul. He came back, we had dinner. And in the evening, naturally, fish was always there. And then we had soup and meat. And then, you know, my father made kiddush. And then my brother with him were singing, you know, zemirot. And I joined them. And my father said, always, shah-- be quiet. But-- and in the morning-- yeah, and I have to mention. Friday, also, I had to take a cholent to the baker. Because for--

speaker: interviewer
text: Say that again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cholent. For supper-- lunch. So I schlepped the big, heavy pot. And my thin brother helped me. But we had to remember the next day which pot is ours. Many times, I brought home not ours. And my father didn't want to eat, because it might not be to kosher for him. So, but I had to go back, and the other party was waiting, and we exchanged the pots.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which holidays did you have with your entire family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, mostly Passover. Passover was such a special family gathering. Sometimes, on the big holidays, on Rosh Hashanah, too. But it depend, and not even every year. It depend, you know. When my grandfather died, and my grandmother died, it was not the same. And so each family kept the holidays for themselves. Only when it was a bar mitzvah, a brit, a new baby came in the family, naturally, it was different. Since my father comes from a family of 11 children, and my mother, 9. So-- and the families were very close, so you can imagine how huge, either from maternal or fraternal side, we had when we got together. And it was really, you know, I never thought that it could be different.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were a small child, what were your games that you played?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the games like other children play. Kick the ball, even I was a girl. We all played with dolls, built houses, had games like merchants, selling sand and beans, and all kinds of things. And then I loved to dress up. And one time, I wanted-- I went to my grandfather from my maternal side-- and it was about 100 miles, 100 kilometers, actually, far away. And my two aunts, who lived in Berlin, they were there. And on the bureau, they had powder and all kinds of creams. I don't think so, I was 10 years old. I put on everything what I saw on my face. And then I went out, and I had a shawl, what she had-- long, and her high heel shoes. And I went out, and I walked. And everybody looked at me and laughed. And I said, they like me. But did I get later, because I messed up the whole house. But I always wanted to be good-looking and dressed. And I envied my schoolgirls. They were all tall. And I was the shrimp, the last in the row.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what your educational goals were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, education-- no. It's very hard to say, because when I was 14 years old, I was kicked out from the school. So what education?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you like? What kind of subjects?

speaker: interviewee
text: I liked everything. I was a-- I was an A student in everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of books did you read?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I read, you know, even before I was 14, what was the newest, you know. I remember this one, [NON-ENGLISH], and [NON-ENGLISH]. Thomas Mann. Victor Hugo. Dickens. Tolstoy. Where I could get, I grab, I read. I was always, somehow, in literature, very, very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in school, was there any anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe off and on, but not that it was such a big deal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe it a little bit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, oh-- they, maybe, you know, I don't know which friend of mine called me, you pretty Jewish, or you short Jewish. But not-- I didn't feel it's like an insult at all. Absolutely not. So I-- I-- I-- I really don't know. I can't recall really that the population in this city. Maybe my parents were differently, but not me. And we never talked in the house about it-- that this Christian, or this anti-Semite. No. No. I-- I think so, it was the cause, too, that we lived there so many centuries. And we knew each family, you know, Christian, too, back way-- you know, back a long time. So it was really closeness. It have-- the whole city was one family, I would say, because we were living there so many, many years. And I can say that. Naturally, you know, later, everything changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the population of your city?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. Maybe 10-- to 20,000. Or maybe less or more. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any recollection of the Jewish cemetery or cemeteries?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do. We had a huge cemetery. Since my mother was buried there, so, once a year, before the holidays, I went to the grave site. And it was a huge-- you know, with child's eyes, huge-- it's very hard to say. Because later on, after the war, when I went back, it didn't seem to me so huge at all. But my-- my family, grandparents, and uncles, and so were buried there. Sure, it was-- I remember, my father belonged to the chevra kadisha. So-- and he was a part of the community, and had some functions-- I don't know what function. But it's, you know, life in the city was nice and comfortable. That's all what I can say. And my family, since they were there-- you felt-- I felt a part of this community and of this city. Whenever I went on vacation visiting my family, I came back, you know, embraced everything, because it was familiar, everything. And I thought I will always live a life like we lived there. Continue in my religion-- less orthodox, because I knew for sure that my father would never allow that I will marry to another religion. And I didn't have any desire to do so even. I didn't envy the Christians. Although I was very curious. I know one time-- it-- I was already 13, I think so. I sneak-- because I slept many times over in my girlfriend's house, in the Christian. And she taught me how beautiful it is on Christmas Eve to go to church at 12 o'clock at night. So I said, you know what, I want to see it. And I went. I just sat, and my head was turned up to the ceiling. Because I wanted to see, you know, those-- those frescoes. Because those were all churches. They were so beautiful, but they were staring at me, pointing at me. Wherever I turned, those eyes came along with me. So I just was sitting and turning my head up to the heavens, like she said.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think they were staring at you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because, you know, I was a Jewish girl. And I was not the same like the last-- rest of the Christian was kneeling, and I didn't kneel.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it impress you, this service?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was always-- I was always impressed with the paintings and with these things, you know, and I detailed them. Each-- each, you know, fold on their clothing. And the hands, you know, they were holding. And the long fingers, you know, embracing the little Jesus.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it fascinated me. But I didn't feel any-- any urge to-- to belong to them. Just curiosity, it was. The only thing. I also always curious about everything. For example?

speaker: interviewee
text: For everything. I wanted to know a lot, I wanted to see a lot, I wanted to be a lot, I wanted to travel. Which I did, every summer, you know, to one and the other [INAUDIBLE] city. And then I was talking in school, you know what I saw, I went to the museum. I saw the big railroad station, the bigger houses, you know, five, six stories, which was not in our city. So I was fascinated with that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were a child, did you have some private lessons besides your school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I didn't have any. I didn't need any private lessons. Because what, you know, was offered in school-- I took everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about music?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I had no talent for it. And I-- I-- I-- I somehow didn't long for it. No-- music, I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to go back. If you would name the synagogue and describe it a little bit in detail?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't name the synagogue-- we had no name. In those cities, it was an orthodox synagogue, or a Reformed, or a Neolog synagogue. So it had no name. It was the orthodox synagogue. It was one building. And a nice, big backyard with fruit trees, and then a playground. And the entrance was a huge hall. Then you came into the synagogue. And side rooms where-- where, you know, children were taught Hebrew.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, you know-- and my father had a seat. Our synagogue had, like, a Sephardic synagogue. In the middle was like-- how you say? You have to go up a few steps, and it was a fence like that around. And the Torah was read from the center of the synagogue.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, you know, where the Torah was kept, the [NON-ENGLISH], or I don't know exactly if I say it right, was on the eastern side with a velvet curtain. And since it was orthodox, the women, we had the balcony upstairs, overlooking, you know, the main synagogue inside. So it was a nice place, very nice. And usually people were talking a lot there. You know, it was not quiet. It was not quiet. They were schmoozing all the time, making business, or arguing, or-- But when the Torah was read, and when they prayed, it was quiet. But it was lots of noise. Lots of noise there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's nice. It was nice. Don't forget-- the orthodox in our-- in our city, I mean, they wore caftans. And [NON-ENGLISH]-- you know, the fur hats? And my father had a fur hat. So it was, you know, you looked from the top down, you saw-- I mean, my imagination-- like, the whole synagogue was velvet with a frame from fur. Because the fur from the top-- when I looked down, you saw fur heads. And when they were close, one to another, it was like one carpet with fur, you know, trimming around. It was, you know, fascinating.
